title: Institutional and Legal Context in Natural Experiments: The Case of State Antitakeover Laws
abstract: We argue and demonstrate empirically that a firm's institutional and legal context has first-order effects in tests that use state antitakeover laws for identification. A priori, the size and direction of a law's effect on a firm's takeover protection depends on (i) other state antitakeover laws, (ii) preexisting firm-level takeover defenses, and (iii) the legal regime as reflected by important court decisions. In addition, (iv) state antitakeover laws are not exogenous for many easily identifiable firms. We show that the inferences from nine prior studies related to nine different outcome variables change substantially when we include controls for these considerations.

article.introduction:
Empirical corporate finance research is plaguedby endogeneity. As Alchian (1950) points out, the economic environment tends to select the combination of firm characteristics—including organization, financing, payout policy, ownership structure, board structure, compensation, and governance—that minimize total production costs. This implies that each firm's characteristics and performance are endogenous outcomes of the competitive process. Researchers nonetheless seek to explore questions of causality. For example, does better governance improve firm value, innovation, or payout policy?
To address endogeneity issues, researchers frequently investigate firm characteristics around regulatory changes or court decisions that exogenously affect firms’ costs or governance. For example, previous studies use banking deregulations to examine innovation and economic growth (Bertrand, Schoar, and Thesmar (2007), Chava et al. (2013)); tax changes to examine firm investment, financing, and payout decisions (Givoly et al. (1992), Dharmapala, Foley, and Forbes (2011), Faulkender and Petersen (2012), Tsoutsoura (2015)); securities and financial reforms to examine firm value, governance, and financing (Coates (2007), Zhang (2007), Atanasov et al. (2010), Iliev (2010), Vig (2013)); legislated subsidies to examine financial constraints (Butler and Cornaggia (2011)); and court decisions to examine the impact of firm governance on firm value, disclosures, and exposure to securities lawsuits (Cohen and Wang (2013), Furchtgott and Partnoy (2015), Crane and Koch (2016), Amihud and Stoyanov (2017), Licht et al. (2017)).1
In this paper, we examine the use of regulatory and legal changes to identify exogenous variation in economic determinants. We focus on an application that has become especially popular, namely state laws that affect firms’ takeover vulnerability. Garvey and Hanka (1999) and Bertrand and Mullainathan (1999) pioneered the use of state antitakeover laws in empirical tests involving firm governance, and in an influential paper, Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003) argue that, among the different types of antitakeover laws, business combination laws have a particularly pronounced effect on firm governance. Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003) thus use business combination laws to examine the impact of firm governance on wages, investment, and productivity. Since then, at least 78 additional published articles and working papers have used business combination and other antitakeover laws to examine the effect of firm governance on operating performance, payout policy, cash holdings, innovation, bondholder returns, stakeholder relations, financial statement informativeness, and other firm characteristics. TableA1lists and provides a brief description of these papers, and SectionI.illustrates the empirical construct used in this literature.
Our main argument is that the institutional and legal environment matters in empirical research of this type. Focusing on state antitakeover laws, particularly business combination laws, we show that the institutional, political-economy, and historical context in which a law is enacted has a large effect on the appropriate specification and interpretation of tests that use legal changes for identification.
Our argument is both theoretical and empirical. SectionsII.toIV.begin by showing that, a priori, the size and direction of an antitakeover law's effect on a firm's takeover protection depends on (i) coverage by a first-generation state antitakeover law or by any of the other four major types of second-generation state antitakeover laws, (ii) preexisting firm-level takeover defenses, and (iii) the legal regime as determined by important court decisions. Coverage by first-generation laws is a particularly important concern for empirical tests that include pre-1982 data because it can reverse the inferences from these tests. An additional concern is that business combination and other state antitakeover laws are not exogenous for, or do not apply to, a substantial number of easily identifiable firms that are incorporated in the states that adopt these laws, either because these firms lobbied for the adoption of these laws or because they opted out of coverage by the laws.
In SectionV., we argue that these institutional and political-economy considerations can be viewed as omitted variable problems for which there exist data-oriented solutions. TableIpresents data on the adoption of first-generation antitakeover laws in 38 states. TableIIpresents state-by-state data on the adoption of all five major types of second-generation state antitakeover laws, including business combination laws. TableIIIpresents data on firms for which the enactment of state antitakeover laws is clearly endogenous. These data supplement those on firms that opted out of coverage that are available in the Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) Governance (formerly, RiskMetrics) database. Cremers and Ferrell (2014) have compiled data on firm-level defenses for a large number of firms for the period 1977 to 1989, which complement the post-1989 data on firm-level defenses available in the ISS database. Together, these data can be used to increase the accuracy and power of empirical tests that use business combination and other state antitakeover laws for identification.
In SectionVI., we use these additional data to demonstrate that legal context influences economic inferences in a wide range of empirical tests. First, we use a generic empirical framework to replicate the basic results in eight prior studies that show that business combination laws are associated with significant changes in seven outcome variables: return on assets (ROA); capital expenditures (Capex); growth in property, plant, and equipment (PPE); asset growth; cash holdings; selling, general, and administrative (SGA) expenses; and leverage. Adding controls for legal context substantially changes the inferences from these tests. For four of the seven outcome variables, the coefficient on business combination laws becomes statistically insignificant. For two of the other three outcome variables, the coefficient on poison pill laws is also statistically and economically significant, suggesting that poison pill laws induce changes in firms’ takeover protections that are at least as important as business combination laws. For four of the seven outcome variables, the primary impact of a business combination law occurs among firms that lobbied for the law and for which the law is clearly not exogenous. Furthermore, the results for all seven outcome variables are insignificant among firms that have firm-level defenses, indicating that the effect of an antitakeover law on a firm's takeover protection is influenced by whether firms have their own defenses.
Next, we exactly replicate the study by Atanassov (2013). Atanassov's main result is that coverage by a business combination law is associated with decreases in a firm's patents and patent citations. When we include controls for other antitakeover laws and relevant court decisions, however, the results for both outcome variables—patents and patent citations—disappear. Instead, we find that patents and patent citations are negatively related to coverage by poison pill laws. They also are negatively related to business combination laws, but only for the small subset of firms that lobbied for the laws and for which the laws are not exogenous. By focusing on the poison pill law result, we can maintain Atanassov's (2013) conclusion that takeover protection is negatively related to a firm's innovative activity. To do so, however, we must drop the assumption that business combination laws identify important exogenous changes in takeover protection.
In the Internet Appendix, we report results of simulations in which we assume that business combination laws have no impact on firm governance or takeover vulnerability and measure the frequencies with which misspecified tests erroneously find that business combination laws are significant determinants of an arbitrary outcome variable.2We find that there exist many underlying structures for which a researcher would mistakenly infer that the passage of a business combination law has a meaningful effect on the outcome variable even though the relation does not in fact exist. In some simulations, tests that erroneously focus only on business combination laws yield significant coefficients with the opposite sign as the (simulated) true impact of takeover protection on the outcome variable.
Our study is related to two contemporaneous papers that seek to correct misperceptions in the finance literature regarding state antitakeover laws. Cain, McKeon, and Solomon (2017) propose that a firm's takeover protection can be measured by an index that is based on a broad range of state laws, court decisions, and firm characteristics. Catan and Kahan (2016) argue that state antitakeover laws impose no incremental takeover protection because all firms have shadow poison pills. SectionVII.examines the Catan and Kahan (2016) argument and concludes that state antitakeover laws’ institutional and legal context is more accurately incorporated using an empirical approach similar to that in SectionVI..
SectionVIII.concludes with three implications of our findings for future research. First, our results and data provide both the motivation and the means to improve the specification of tests that use antitakeover laws to identify changes in firms’ takeover defenses. Second, our results, along with those of Cain, McKeon, and Solomon (2017) and Catan and Kahan (2016), highlight the paucity of empirical research on whether and how individual firm- and state-level takeover defenses affect firms’ takeover protection and how these defenses interact. Third, our findings support explicit recognition of the importance of inductive inference in empirical corporate finance research, especially regarding legal changes to firms’ takeover protections. Inductive inference has been correctly criticized for its invitation to data mining and ex post theorizing (e.g., Allen (2001)). Arthur (1994), Clower (1994), and Johnson (1996), however, argue that economics is primarily an inductive science. Until future research better establishes which laws, court decisions, and firm-level defenses best identify cross-sectional or time series variation in firms’ takeover protections, one reasonable approach is to consider inferences that are transparently inductive in nature. An example of such an approach is our inference that Atanassov's (2013) conclusion about the impact of takeover protection on firm innovation is supported by the data, but only if we use poison pill laws and not business combination laws to identify changes in takeover protection.
article.I. The Empirical Construct Using Busines:
Figure1illustrates the empirical construct pioneered by Garvey and Hanka (1999) and Bertrand and Mullainathan (1999,2003). Using data from 1976 through 1995, Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003) track the levels of several key variables, including worker wages, investment, productivity, and profitability. They hypothesize that these firm characteristics are affected by the quality of the firm's governance, and that governance is negatively affected by the degree to which incumbent managers are protected from unsolicited takeover bids. Citing research by Romano (1987), Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003) also argue that business combination laws are exogenous for virtually all covered firms. The adoption of a business combination law therefore represents an exogenous increase in the level of takeover protection and decrease in the quality of firm governance for firms incorporated in the state that adopts the law.
Figure1illustrates this research design for firms incorporated in Massachusetts, which adopted a business combination law on July 18, 1989. A researcher compares the characteristic of interest—for example, firm investment or innovation—before July 18, 1989 and after July 18, 1989, and attributes any change in the characteristic to the (exogenous) adoption of the business combination law. Different states adopted business combination laws at different times, bolstering the claim that the test is unlikely to be driven by contemporaneous changes that affect all firms from a single state. Using this approach, Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003) find that the passage of a business combination law is associated with an increase in workers’ wages, decreases in the rates with which old plants are destroyed and new plants are created, and decreases in productivity and profitability. They infer that these changes are caused by the increase in takeover protection afforded by coverage under business combination laws.
article.II. Institutional Considerations:
The identification strategy illustrated in Figure1works as long as firms’ takeover protections were low from 1976 to July 18, 1989, when Massachusetts's business combination law was passed. It turns out, however, that, for nearly half this period, most firms’ takeover protections were extremely high, not low. This is because, until 1982, firms in Massachusetts and 37 other states were covered by first-generation state antitakeover laws. TableIlists the states that adopted first-generation laws and the dates these laws became effective. Using these adoption dates, 90% of firms in the ISS Governance database that were covered by business combination laws were previously covered by first-generation laws, including 73% of all firms not incorporated in Delaware.
First-generation antitakeover laws were effectively invalidated by a U.S. Supreme Court decision inEdgar v. MITE Corp. on June 23, 1982.3Prior to theMITEdecision, these laws provided managers with aggressive defenses against unwanted takeover bids. As described by Jarrell and Bradley (1980), most first-generation laws contained four types of provisions. The first provision required that a bidder file disclosure statements with the state securities commissioner and send copies of the disclosure statements to the target company. The required disclosures were more extensive than those required by section 13(D) of the 1968 Williams Act, as they required that the bidder provide detailed information about itself and its plans for the target firm. The disclosures also had to be filed 10 to 30 days before the bidder announced its tender offer for the target. Such a long advanced warning period provided target firm managers time to implement defensive measures and alert investors to the prospect of a bid. To the extent that the bidder sought to gain from a toehold investment (e.g., see Walkling (1985)), the effect was to both increase the bidder's costs and decrease its expected gain from the acquisition.
The second provision common to most first-generation laws was a requirement to hold the tender offer open for longer periods than required under the 1968 Williams Act. Two states (Massachusetts and Michigan) required tender periods of 60 days, and most laws created administrative review procedures that allowed for repeated extensions of the tender offer period. As Romano (1993, p. 852) notes, “…state review tended to be extremely protracted.” Lengthy tender periods, especially periods that can be extended repeatedly, increase the bidder's expected cost as well as the probability of competitive bids from other bidders (e.g., see Jarrell and Bradley (1980)).
The third provision in most first-generation laws created administrative overview of the tender offer by the state's securities commissioner. Target companies could demand a hearing with the commissioner to seek an injunction to delay the tender offer. In most (22) states, the commissioner could seek an injunction in state court if the commissioner deemed the offer to be illegal, and in one state (Virginia) the commissioner could unilaterally declare the tender offer illegal. It is reasonable to believe that state elected or appointed officials would be more sympathetic to the complaints of the target firm's managers than those of the bidder's managers, who typically would have fewer local political connections.
The fourth provision common to most first-generation laws made the bidding firm liable for damages arising from the takeover offer, and in some cases established criminal penalties—including imprisonment for bidding firm managers—for violations of the disclosure and administrative procedures established by the law. The prospect of such penalties, or of fighting them through the legal process, represented a significant cost that could deter many prospective bids.
Consistent with the view that first-generation laws provided strong takeover protection, Jarrell and Bradley (1980) find that takeover premiums increased significantly after the imposition of first-generation laws, and Smiley (1981) finds that these laws were associated with a significant decrease in hostile takeover bids. The effect of first-generation laws’ strong antitakeover protections is illustrated in Figure2, which continues the example for firms incorporated in Massachusetts. Massachusetts adopted a first-generation state antitakeover law on May 22, 1976. For firms incorporated in Massachusetts, the level of takeover protection is very high from this date through June 23, 1982, when the Supreme Court issued its decision inEdgar v. MITE Corp. For empirical tests that use data prior to 1983, observations from the pre-MITEperiod should be coded as having the highest level of takeover protection, not the lowest. Note that the effect is to substantially muddy, and possibly reverse, the interpretation of many tests. Suppose, for example, that we observe that Capex decreases from the pre-1989 period to the post-1989 period. Roughly half of the pre-1989 period has the highest level of takeover protection. Should we infer that Capex decreased because the firm's takeover protections were relatively high after 1989, or because they were relatively low?
Almost immediately after theMITEdecision in June 1982, states began adopting second-generation antitakeover laws and firms began adopting new firm-level takeover defenses. Both developments complicate tests based on state antitakeover laws, in particular business combination laws, even for tests that are limited to a post-1982 sample period. Since 1982, when Ohio passed the first control share acquisition law, 43 states have adopted at least 157 second-generation antitakeover laws. TableIIlists the five most prominent types of second-generation antitakeover laws and the dates these laws became effective.4In addition to business combination laws, these laws include control share acquisition, fair price, poison pill, and directors' duties (constituency) laws. The Appendix contains descriptions of each of these laws.
All antitakeover laws have the potential to increase a firm's protection from unsolicited takeover bids. However, many researchers assume that only business combination laws offer meaningful takeover protection. For example, Francis et al. (2010, p. 128) observe that “… Business Combination laws … are considered in the extant literature as the most stringent state antitakeover laws,” Sauvagnat (2013, p. 15) argues that business combination laws “… are the most stringent among the second and third generation antitakeover laws,” and Atanassov (2013, p. 1101) observes that “Academics and practitioners alike argue that, among different types of antitakeover laws, Business Combination laws increase managerial entrenchment the most.”
These claims, which help justify research designs that ignore the other types of antitakeover laws, appear to be based on Bertrand and Mullainathan's (2003, p. 1047) assertion that “The most stringent of the second- and third-generation laws were known as business combination laws, which will be the focus of our study … [the] other laws are thought to be, at best, marginally effective.” To support their claim, Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003) cite an event study result in Karpoff and Malatesta (1989) in which news of a business combination law is associated with a larger stock price decline than control share acquisition or fair price laws. Bertrand and Mullainathan's (2003) argument has thus become the foundation of the now-popular focus on business combination laws and the practice of ignoring the takeover protection afforded by other types of antitakeover laws.
This argument, however, has three drawbacks. First, it is difficult to build an a priori case that business combination laws offer uniquely strong takeover protection while the other laws offer negligible protection. Second, the available empirical evidence does not support the argument that business combination laws are the most restrictive. Poison pill laws, for example, are associated with larger price drops among affected firms. Third, a researcher who relies on event studies to justify the use of business combination laws faces an even more troublesome problem, as most of these studies indicate that firm-level defenses confound identification strategies that rely on antitakeover laws. The following sections describe each of these concerns in more detail.
On a purely theoretical basis, it is not evident which type of antitakeover law offers the greatest protection from unsolicited takeovers.5Poison pill laws, for example, explicitly authorize firms to adopt poison pill takeover defenses. Coates (2000), Daines (2001), Daines and Klausner (2001), and Bebchuk, Coates, and Subramanian (2002) argue that poison pills are particularly effective takeover deterrents, and Cremers and Ferrell (2014) conclude that poison pills are the most effective of all the takeover defenses in Gompers, Ishii, and Metrick's (2003) G-index. A 1985 Delaware Supreme Court decision validated Delaware firms’ use of so-called flip-over poison pills. But in many other states, poison pills had ambiguous legal status until passage of a poison pill law. If we accept the argument that poison pills are particularly effective takeover deterrents, an a priori case can be made that poison pill laws are the most stringent type of antitakeover law.6
Alternatively, directors’ duties laws may provide the greatest takeover protection because they direct corporate directors to consider the interests of all firm stakeholders, including employees and local communities, in addition to shareholders’ interests. The effect of this directive is to provide legal protection to board decisions that do not maximize shareholder value. For example, a board covered by a directors’ duties law can reject a tender offer that is attractive to shareholders by claiming that the change in control would hurt employees or the local community. Such a broad directive to consider many constituents provides legal cover for boards that wish to “just say no” to unsolicited takeover bids (Del Guercio, Seery, and Woidtke (2008)). For example, Bebchuk and Ferrell (1999, p. 1180) note that directors’ duties laws, “Arguably [provide] managers with an even greater ability to formulate a legally acceptable reason not to dismantle a poison pill or refrain from whatever other defensive maneuvers they might wish to engage in.”
The provisions of control share acquisition laws also illustrate the weak theoretical foundation for the claim that business combination laws are the “most stringent.” A control share acquisition law strips a bidder's voting rights unless a majority of other shareholders vote to restore such rights. Incumbent managers’ voting rights, in contrast, are not affected. This requires that a bidder obtain supermajority support from disinterested shares to complete an acquisition, which increases the risk of failure and likely deters many unsolicited bids in the first place.
A business combination law, by comparison, imposes no hurdles on the bidder's acquisition of shares. Rather, it imposes a two- to five-year delay (depending on the specific law) on some kinds of transactions between the bidder and the firm, including a merger or significant asset sale. This forced delay can increase the bidder's costs, and some evidence indicates that business combinations are effective takeover deterrents (e.g., Subramanian, Herscovici, and Barbetta (2010a)). But, on a purely theoretical basis, it is not obvious that business combination laws provide more takeover protection than poison pill authorizations, the expansion of directors’ duties to include noninvestor stakeholders, or control share acquisition requirements.7
Although there is little theoretical support for focusing only on business combination laws, Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003, footnote 8) argue that there is empirical support: “Event study evidence has borne out this belief [that business combination laws are the most stringent], showing that business combination laws resulted in the biggest stock price drop (Karpoff and Malatesta (1989)).” Consistent with this argument, table 5 in the Karpoff and Malatesta (1989) paper shows that the mean two-day forecast error upon the revelation of a business combination law is –0.467%, with az-statistic of –2.697, for firms incorporated in the state of adoption. The mean forecast errors for control share acquisition and fair price laws, while negative, are not statistically significant.
There are three problems, however, with this argument. First, the broader evidence is mixed on whether business combination laws are associated with negative stock price reactions. Mahla (1991) and Giroud and Mueller (2010) report statistically significant results, similar to those in Karpoff and Malatesta (1989). But Broner (1987), Pugh and Jahera (1990), and Jahera and Pugh (1991) report statistically insignificant stock price reactions to business combination laws. Second, the average stock price reactions to business combination laws, even in studies in which they are statistically significant, are not significantly different from the stock price reactions to control share acquisition or fair price laws (e.g., see Table 5 in Karpoff and Malatesta (1989)). So, any claim that business combination laws are the “most stringent” must be based on point estimates without regard for standard errors of the estimates.
Third, point estimates of the average stock price reactions to different types of antitakeover laws indicate that business combination laws are not the “most stringent”—that distinction goes instead to poison pill laws. In the same table that reports the stock price reaction to business combination laws, Karpoff and Malatesta (1989) report that the average stock price reaction for firms that are newly affected by poison pill laws is 2.4 times larger in magnitude (–1.125%), and statistically significant at the 5% level. Similarly, Ryngaert and Netter (1988) report a mean forecast error of –1.94% (t-statistic = –2.11) for the three-day window surrounding the first media report of the Ohio poison pill law. If we were to use point estimates from event study results to identify the “most stringent” antitakeover law, the evidence would suggest that researchers use poison pill laws to identify their empirical tests. Consistent with this argument, the empirical tests in SectionVI.indicate that poison pill laws are significantly related to outcomes such as operating performance, SGA expenses, leverage, and innovative activity.
In summary, there is no solid theoretical or empirical basis for the assertion that “business combination laws [are] the most stringent…” or that other types of antitakeover laws are irrelevant. Rather, the effect of a business combination law on covered firms’ takeover protection is most likely affected by the presence of other types of antitakeover laws.
Figure3uses the laws adopted in Massachusetts to illustrate the effect of other antitakeover laws on tests that rely on business combination laws for identification. Before its business combination law was adopted on July 18, 1989, Massachusetts adopted a control share acquisition law on July 22, 1987. In Figure3, this is represented as an increase in the level of takeover protection for covered firms. It is plausible that the 1989 business combination law provided additional takeover protection over and above the 1987 law. Reflecting this possibility, Figure3is drawn such that the passage of both the control share acquisition law and business combination law increased Massachusetts firms’ takeover protection. A correct specification would account for the increased takeover protection afforded by each of these laws—a task facilitated by TableII, which summarizes states’ adoption of all major types of antitakeover laws.
TableIIreveals that it is typical for states that adopt business combination laws to have preexisting antitakeover laws or to adopt other types of antitakeover laws at the same time they adopt business combination laws. Only 2 of the 36 states with business combination laws—Delaware and Texas—passed their business combination laws on a standalone basis without at least one other antitakeover law already in place. Of the 4,141 firms in the ISS Governance database, 20.7% are incorporated in states in which a business combination law was preceded by at least one other second-generation law. An additional 25.2% of firms are incorporated in states in which business combination laws were adopted in the same legislative bill as at least one other type of second-generation antitakeover law.8
Relying on differences in stock price reactions to identify which type of law is “most stringent” presents an even more important concern. In Tables 3 and 4 of their paper, Karpoff and Malatesta (1989) report that state antitakeover laws are associated with statistically significant stock price effects only among firms that do not have preexisting firm-level takeover defenses. For firms with preexisting takeover defenses, the mean stock price reaction is close to 0 and statistically insignificant. This result, which has been supported in several studies, implies that firm-level defenses are substitutes for state antitakeover laws in their provision of antitakeover protection.9
Figure4illustrates the effect of firm-level defenses on takeover protection for one Massachusetts firm, Cullinane Database Systems, Inc. Cullinane adopted a supermajority vote requirement on July 26, 1982. If firm-level defenses are substitutes for state antitakeover law protection, the 1989 adoption of Massachusetts’s business combination law had little impact on the level of Cullinane's takeover protection. Rather, most of the increase in takeover protection occurred in 1982, when Cullinane adopted its supermajority vote requirement. Stated differently, if we want to rely on event study evidence to support a focus on any single type of antitakeover law (e.g., business combination laws or poison pill laws), we must also accept the event study evidence that such laws increase takeover protection primarily for firms without prior firm-level defenses.
Figure4illustrates an extreme scenario in which the business combination law offers no incremental takeover protection for Cullinane. Even if the actual effect is not so extreme, many firms deploy takeover defenses at the firm level and are affected by this concern (e.g., see Gompers, Ishii, and Metrick (2003) for information on the number of firms with firm-level defenses). Karpoff and Malatesta (1989) report that 24.5% of firms in their sample through 1987 had at least one takeover defense when their states adopted antitakeover laws. To obtain a lower bound estimate of the number of firms that had firm-level defenses when their states adopted business combination laws, we combine Cremers and Ferrell's (2014) data on takeover defenses from 1977 to 1989 with the ISS Governance database for the 1990 to 2012 period. A total of 611 (14.75%) of the 4,141 firms in the ISS Governance database had at least one firm-level defense in place when the firm's state of incorporation adopted a business combination law. This fraction is a lower bound estimate because the Cremers and Ferrell (2014) data for the 1977 to 1989 period cover only 1,346 firms, not all 4,141 firms in the ISS database.10This implies that Figure4illustrates the general pattern of takeover protection for a significant number of firms.
One who defends an empirical approach that relies exclusively on business combination laws might argue that the presence of other types of state antitakeover laws (Figure3) or firm-level defenses (Figure4) merely works to bias the experiment against finding significant results. If the test yields significant results, does that not indicate that the results are robust?
The existence of first-generation antitakeover laws constitutes the most obvious concern with such an argument, at least for tests that include pre-1983 data. In such cases, the periods after business combination laws are passed are more accurately characterized as periods of moderate to low takeover protections, not high takeover protections.
Even for tests that exclude pre-1983 observations, however, low-power tests remain a concern, for three reasons. First, low-power tests may fail to uncover genuine patterns in the data. Thus, researchers may find insignificant results even when there is an underlying relation between governance and a firm characteristic. Second, as Burgstahler (1987) points out, low-power tests are relatively uninformative even when the test statistics are significant. And third, tests that ignore other state antitakeover laws and firm-level defenses cannot discern whether changes in firm characteristics (if any) are due to the business combination law or other differences in firm-level defenses. Danielson and Karpoff (1998) show that the use of firm-level defenses frequently clusters with coverage by state antitakeover laws. Many states that adopt business combination laws also adopt other types of antitakeover laws, indicating that coverage by omitted state laws is also correlated with business combination law coverage. So, whether the correlated omitted variable is coverage by another type of state antitakeover law or use of a firm-level defense, tests that rely solely on business combination law coverage suffer an omitted variable problem that can lead to biased estimators (e.g., see Roberts and Whited (2012)).
article.III. Court Decisions that Affect Firms’:
In addition to other state antitakeover laws and firm-level defenses, the incremental impact of an antitakeover law depends on the legal environment. We identify four court rulings that had large effects on the takeover protections offered by antitakeover laws. The Internet Appendix also reports on the effects of several additional court decisions in our empirical replication tests.
Between 1978 and 1982, at least 32 lower courts issued decisions on the constitutionality of various states’ first-generation laws. These decisions were mixed, with 17 ruling that some aspect of a first-generation law was unconstitutional and 15 upholding the laws.11The matter was settled on June 23, 1982, when the U.S. Supreme Court ruled inEdgar v. MITEthat the Illinois first-generation antitakeover law was unconstitutional on the grounds that it imposed an indirect burden on interstate commerce.12The ruling effectively invalidated all other 37 first-generation laws as well. As illustrated in Figure2, theMITEdecision had the effect of immediately decreasing the level of takeover protection for firms that previously were covered by a first-generation law. In our empirical tests, we incorporate the impact of theMITEdecision in the coding of our variable for coverage by a first-generation law. This variable takes a value of one when a state adopts a first-generation law and is flipped back to 0 for all firms in all states in 1982.
After theEdgar v. MITEdecision in 1982, state legislatures crafted new antitakeover laws that met the constitutional objections to the first-generation laws. Litigation involving the legality of one of these second-generation laws reached the U.S. Supreme Court in a case involving Indiana's control share acquisition law inCTS Corp. v. Dynamics Corp. of America.13Both the District Court and Court of Appeals ruled that the federal Williams Act preempted the Indiana law. But on April 21, 1987, the U.S. Supreme Court overturned these prior rulings and ruled that the Indiana law is constitutional. The Court rejected the claim that the Indiana law was preempted by federal law and concluded that the Indiana law actually furthered the goal of the Williams Act to protect shareholders because it allows shareholders to evaluate the fairness of a tender offer collectively. Further, the court rejected the argument that the law unduly burdened interstate commerce. Until theCTSdecision, the level of takeover protection afforded by control share acquisition laws was uncertain and ambiguous.
WhileCTSaffirmed the constitutionality of control share acquisition laws, the constitutionality of business combination laws was not established until a ruling by the U.S. Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals inAmanda Acquisition Corp. v. Universal Foods Corp. on May 24, 1989.14In our empirical applications in SectionVI., we find that business combination laws in and of themselves are not associated with meaningful changes in most outcome variables. In two tests, however, the interaction of coverage by a business combination law with theAmandadecision is significant, suggesting that the important identifying event is not the prior passage of a business combination law, but rather the first appellate-level court decision that upheld a business combination law.
A fourth important court decision affirmed the legality of poison pills for Delaware firms. On November 19, 1985, the Delaware Supreme Court ruled inMoran v. Household International, Inc. that Household International's adoption of a poison pill was a legitimate exercise of its managers’ business judgment.15Delaware Court decisions frequently apply de facto even to non-Delaware corporations. In SectionC., we examine the effects on our empirical inferences if we assume that theMorancase validated the use of poison pills for non-Delaware firms. In our main tests, however, we assume that theMoranruling upheld Delaware firms’ rights to deploy poison pills and that the legal status of poison pills remained unclear for firms incorporated in other states. Consistent with this assumption, Lipton and Steinberger (2003, pp. 6–76) and Catan and Kahan (2016, p. 636) cite court decisions in which poison pills were invalidated for companies from New York, New Jersey, Georgia, Wisconsin, Colorado, and Virginia. Each of these states passed a law to establish firms’ rights to deploy poison pills soon after its related court decision. In total, 35 states have adopted poison pill laws. In our empirical tests in SectionVI., we treat these poison pill laws as similar to the Delaware Court's decision in theMorancase. Firms in 36 states therefore have explicit legal protection to adopt and use poison pills, either via statute or, in the case of Delaware firms, via theMorandecision.
article.IV. Political Economy and Selection Issu:
In SectionsIII.andIV.above, we discuss how other state antitakeover laws, firm-level defenses, and court decisions can affect the use of business combination laws for identification. Each of these is essentially a problem of omitted variables, and the data in TablesIandII, as well as the ISS Governance database, can help address these problems. In this section, we discuss an additional concern, namely whether coverage by a state antitakeover law is exogenous for all firms.
The use of state antitakeover laws to identify empirical tests relies on the assumption that they are imposed exogenously on the covered firms. To justify this assumption, Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003) cite Romano's (1987) conclusion that most of these laws did not result from broad-based coalitions of firms that lobbied for them. Werner and Coleman (2015) argue, however, that antitakeover laws are strongly affected by corporate lobbying. Consistent with this argument, Karpoff and Malatesta (1989) identify 19 antitakeover laws that were passed at the behest of specific companies through 1989. Using more current data, Gartman (2000) reports that 49 state antitakeover laws in 23 states were passed in part because they were promoted by at least 46 firms.
These 49 laws and the associated motivating firms are listed in TableIII. In most cases the motivating firm was the target of an actual or rumored takeover bid, and in most cases the state legislature moved quickly to pass antitakeover legislation upon news of the actual or potential bid. For example, Mesa Petroleum announced plans to buy a $15 million stake in The Boeing Company on July 28, 1987. The very next day, news reports indicated that the Washington state legislature would consider antitakeover legislation to protect Boeing from the threat of outside takeover. The legislature met in a special session and adopted Washington's business combination law within two weeks of Mesa's announced toehold investment in Boeing. Similar legislative histories, although not always with the same alacrity, characterize business combination laws adopted in Kentucky (Ashland Oil), Minnesota (Dayton-Hudson), New Jersey (Schering-Plough), and Wisconsin (G. Heilman Brewing), as well as the other antitakeover laws listed in TableIII.
Romano's (1987) argument implies that antitakeover laws do not result from broad-based coalitions of firms, so for most firms the laws can be treated as exogenous. For the firms and laws listed in TableIII, however, the assumption of exogeneity is clearly inaccurate. Dayton-Hudson's coverage by the Minnesota business combination law, for example, is as endogenous as Dayton-Hudson's coverage by its own poison pill. The forces that give rise to both types of takeover protection reflect Dayton-Hudson's unique agency costs, governance, and performance characteristics. This implies that the assumption of exogeneity is violated for firms that helped motivate specific antitakeover laws. In SectionV., we discuss two simple approaches to treat the endogenous adoption of these 49 laws for the 46 motivating firms. The results in SectionVI.indicate that the endogenous nature of the laws for these 46 firms has a material effect on empirical inferences.
The exogeneity assumption also could be violated for many firms because most antitakeover laws include opt-out provisions. For example, Illinois corporations can opt out of coverage by the Illinois business combination law through a charter amendment or bylaw that is supported by a majority of the shares not held by an outside bidder. Empirical researchers can improve their tests by recognizing that many firms do, in fact, opt out of their state laws’ coverage. The ISS Governance database indicates that 445 (10.75%) of the 4,141 firms represented in the database opted out of coverage by at least one state antitakeover law for at least one year during the 1990 to 2012 period. These include 320 firms that opted out of control share acquisition laws, 158 firms that opted out of coverage by business combination laws, 72 firms that opted out of fair price laws, 37 firms that opted out of directors’ duties laws, and 3 firms that opted out of poison pill laws. In our tests below, we code these firm-years as not subject to the provisions of the applicable state antitakeover law.16
Two considerations, however, indicate that the assumption of exogeneity remains generally valid for firms that remain covered by state antitakeover laws. First, most firms’ managers, whose private benefits are protected by takeover defenses, are unlikely to initiate an attempt to opt out of coverage. As noted by Hannes (2005) and Johnson, Karpoff, and Yi (2017), few firms remove their takeover defenses. Coates (2000) argues that this is because their managers desire more, not fewer, defenses. Moreover, Kahan and Klausner (1996) suggest that status quo bias causes firms to maintain their current takeover defenses, which further decreases the likelihood that many firms will opt out of coverage. Second, even for firms that do seek to opt out of coverage, the opt-out does not take effect immediately. To opt out of the Ohio business combination law, for example, requires a two-thirds majority of the outstanding shares and two-thirds of the outstanding shares not owned by a 10% stockholder. Even then, the opt-out does not take effect for 12 months and does not apply to a control transaction with a shareholder who owned 10% before the opt-out amendment passed. This implies that, even for firms that opt out of coverage, they are subject to the law during the delay period.
An additional reason coverage by a state antitakeover law may be endogenous is that firms choose their states of incorporation and can reincorporate to states that offer takeover defenses that best suit their needs. Catan and Kahan (2016) argue that the option to reincorporate implies that state antitakeover law coverage is not exogenous for many, if not most, firms. However, we do not focus on this potential source of endogeneity in our replication tests for three reasons. First, virtually all state antitakeover laws have opt-out provisions, as discussed above. Firms are likely to find it less costly to opt out of coverage than to avoid coverage by reincorporating to a different state, and our tests already control for firms that opt out of coverage by an antitakeover law. Second, any initiative to reincorporate to avoid coverage by an antitakeover law must come from managers. As observed above, most firms’ takeover defenses are extremely rigid, perhaps due to a stalemate between managers and shareholders (Coates (2000)) or status quo bias (Kahan and Klausner (1996)), implying that firms do not easily reincorporate to change their antitakeover law coverage. Third, the evidence does not indicate that a large number of firms reincorporate to avoid or acquire coverage by state antitakeover laws (e.g., see Cremers and Sepe (2015)). Cain, McKeon, and Solomon (2017), for example, report that the large majority of reincorporations in their sample are to the firm's headquarters state, Delaware, or Nevada. Few reincorporations are to states such as Pennsylvania, which Gartman (2000), Szewczyk and Tsetsekos (1992), and others argue has the most manager-friendly takeover environment. Nonetheless, the fact that firms have the option to reincorporate underscores one result from our empirical replications, which show that the relations between business combination laws and the outcome variables are frequently different for firms for which the laws are clearly not exogenous than for the overall sample of firms.
article.V. Institutional and Legal Context Conce: The estimation challenge raised by multiple state antitakeover laws, court cases, and firm-level defenses can be viewed as an omitted variables problem. Further, the political economy of state antitakeover laws, including the fact that some firms lobbied for the laws while others opted out of coverage, raise endogeneity and selection concerns. The statistical issues raised by each of these concerns are well understood. Meyer (1995), for example, lists omitted variables, political economy considerations, and selection as three threats to the internal validity of empirical tests that seek to draw inferences about causation.
article.VI. The Importance of Institutional and:
This section examines the importance of institutional and legal context in empirical tests using seven outcome variables that have been examined in eight prior studies: ROA, Capex, PPE growth, asset growth, cash holdings, SGA expenses, and leverage.18For each outcome variable, we compare the results from estimating the short regression (equation1) with the results from the full model (equation4). To match the sample period in Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003) and Giroud and Mueller (2010), we use annual Compustat data from 1976 to 1995 and exclude financial firms and utilities. All regressions include the control variables used by Giroud and Mueller (2010), which include the natural log of the book value of assets (size), size squared, firm age, and firm age squared. We winsorize all continuous control and outcome variables at the 0.5% and 99.5% levels.
In addition to the control variables, we include firm, state (of location)-year, and industry-year fixed effects in all tests in TablesIVandV. Such high-dimensional fixed effects decrease the likelihood of finding a significant coefficient forBC. While most prior studies in this area only include fixed effects for firm, industry, and/or year separately, we include interacted fixed effects to increase confidence that our finding of a significantBCcoefficient in the short regression is not attributable to unobserved sources of heterogeneous variation related to the firm's industry, location, or year of observation. Gormley and Matsa (2014,2016) show that the use of high-dimensional fixed effects is an effective control for such common factors.
TableIVreports the results of tests in which the vectorLin equation4includes first-generation laws and the four other most common types of second-generation laws (poison pill laws, control share acquisition laws, director duties laws, and fair price laws), plus two time-related dummy variables that reflect the resolution of uncertainty about the legal status of second-generation laws. The interactionCS×CTSequals 1 for all firms covered by control share acquisition laws for observations beginning in 1987, when the U.S. Supreme Court upheld Indiana's control share acquisition law, andBC×Amandaequals 1 for all firms covered by business combination laws after the U.S. Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals upheld Wisconsin's business combination law in 1989. Finally,BC×MFis the interaction of business combination law coverage with a dummy variable equal to 1 for firms that helped motivate the business combination laws, as identified in TableIII. There are 25 such motivating firms, affiliated with 356 firm-years in the sample. Again, we do not include a separate control forMFbecause the tests already include firm fixed effects.
Model (1a) reports the results from the short regression (equation1) in which ROA is the dependent variable. The coefficient on the business combination law dummy variable is –0.017, significant at the 10% level. This result is consistent with Bertrand and Mullainathan's (2003) and with Giroud and Mueller's (2010) baseline finding (without an industry concentration interaction) that business combination law coverage is associated with a decrease in firm operating performance.
Model (1b) reports the ROA results using the full model (equation4). Here, the coefficient onBCis smaller and statistically insignificant. In contrast, ROA is significantly negatively related to coverage by a first-generation state law, poison pill law, and control share acquisition law, as well as to coverage by a business combination law after the 1989Amandacourt decision. These results do not necessarily refute the inference that ROA is negatively related to coverage by antitakeover laws, but they do indicate that business combination laws provide poor identification for tests that seek exogenous changes in a firm's takeover vulnerability. In unreported tests, we confirm that the OVB in Model (1) is negative because coverage by a business combination law is positively related to three variables (poison pill law, control share acquisition law, and coverage by a business combination law after the 1989Amandacourt decision) that are negatively related to ROA.
In Model (1b), the coefficient onBC×MFis positive and significant at the 1% level. This result is consistent with the view that business combination laws are optimal for the firms that lobbied for the laws, or at least that the laws’ effects are different for the motivating firms compared with other firms.
Models (2) through (7) in TableIVreport results for the six other outcome variables. In the short regression tests, business combination law coverage is significantly related to all of these variables. In three cases (Capex, PPE growth, and cash holdings), however, the business combination coefficient is insignificant in the full model tests. Overall, the OVB from estimating the short regression instead of the full model leads to a fundamentally different inference about the effect of business combination laws for four of the seven outcome variables examined in TableIV.
The coefficients on our indicators for poison pill laws andBC×MFare significantly related to three and four of the seven outcome variables in TableIV, respectively. The poison pill law results are consistent with our previous observation that poison pill laws are associated with a larger average share price reaction than business combination laws. These results indicate that researchers seeking measures of exogenous variation in takeover vulnerability should consider poison pill laws. In three of the four tests in whichBC×MFis significant, its coefficient has the opposite sign asBCin the corresponding short equation. For example, Giroud and Mueller (2010) argue that an increase in takeover protection will lead to entrenched managers, less efficient deployment of resources, and an increase in SGA expenses. This interpretation is consistent with the positive coefficients in Model (6b) for business combination and poison pill laws. The coefficient onBC×MF, in contrast, is negative and significant, indicating that firms that lobbied for their business combination laws experienced a decrease in SGA expenses. These results indicate that the effects of an increase in state-level takeover protection are frequently different for motivating firms than for other firms.
We use a simple approach to examine the impact of firm-level defenses on the empirical results. First, we combine data from the ISS Governance database, which reports on firm-level defenses for S&P 1500 firms dating back to 1990, with data on firms’ G-index values dating back to 1978 that were kindly provided to us by Martijn Cremers and Allen Ferrell. The Cremers and Ferrell (2014) data provided to us do not identify firms’ individual takeover defenses, but when combined with our data on state antitakeover laws, allow us to identify firms that have at least one firm-level defense in any given year. These sources indicate that 2,045 of the 10,213 firms in the Compustat database over the 1976 to 1995 period had one or more firm-level takeover defenses. TableVreports results from the short regression (equation1) for each of the seven outcome variables using only these 2,045 firms. This is a crude approach to controlling for the impact of firm-level defenses, but it is similar to the empirical approach used by Karpoff and Malatesta (1989) and Szewczyk and Tsetsekos (1992) to examine the valuation effects of state antitakeover laws among firms with preexisting firm-level defenses.
Even this crude approach indicates that firm-level defenses have a large effect on the empirical relation between business combination laws and the various outcome variables. In TableV, the coefficient onBCis statistically insignificant in all seven regressions. This differs from the results using the short regression (equation1) in TableIV. All seven outcome variables are significantly related toBCin the short regression when we include firms without firm-level defenses. Among firms with firm-level defenses, however, this result disappears even without considering our other institutional and political economy variables. These results are consistent with the situation characterized by Figure4, where we see that the incremental impact of a state antitakeover law is smaller among firms that already have firm-level defenses.
The results in TablesIVandVillustrate the importance of our political economy concerns in a generic test setting that uses a sampling interval and control variables that are similar to those in Giroud and Mueller (2010). We do not claim, however, to have exactly replicated any prior tests. Our tests regarding ROA, for example, are similar in nature to those in Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003), but are not exact replications because we do not have the U.S. census and plant-level data that were available to Bertrand and Mullainathan. In this section, we demonstrate the importance of our institutional and political economy concerns in a setting in which we can exactly replicate a previous empirical result because Julian Atanassov graciously provided us with the data for his 2013Journal of Financepaper on corporate innovation.
Atanassov (2013) finds that antitakeover laws, particularly business combination laws, are associated with a decrease in both the number of patents filed by affected firms and the number of citations to these new patents. Atanassov infers that the extra takeover protection afforded by business combination laws is associated with a decrease in both the quantity and quality of these firms’ innovative activity. He concludes that antitakeover laws exacerbate the managerial agency problem as managers respond to an increase in takeover protection by pursuing activities that are less challenging and less valuable than new innovation. Atanassov's (2013) results thus offer an important contribution to the debate on whether takeover protections facilitate or impede innovative activity.19
Panel A of TableVIreproduces Atanassov's (2013) main result regarding new patent activity, as reported in TableIIIof his paper. The dependent variable,Ln(1 +Pat)t+3, is the natural log of the number of new patents filed by a firm in a given year divided by the mean number of patents in the same year. Atanassov (2013) uses the number of patents filed three years in the future to capture the time lag between innovation investments and the resulting patents. As in TablesIVandV, the key variable of interest isBC, an indicator variable equal to 1 if a business combination law is in place in the firm's state of incorporation in a given year. Control variables, which are not reported in our table, include firm sales, leverage, age, profitability, asset tangibility, and industry concentration. As reported in Model (1) and by Atanassov (2013), the coefficient on coverage by a business combination law is –0.014 and is statistically significant at the 1% level.
For our replication tests we use a subset of 85,803 firm-years in Atanassov's (2013) sample for which we can identify states of incorporation using Compustat. As reported in Model (2), the coefficient onBCfalls to –0.012 for this sample, but is still significant at the 1% level. In Model (3), we make two changes to account for the fact that the laws do not apply to all firms. First, we account for the fact that some firms opted out of business combination law coverage by coding these firm-years as not covered by the business combination law. This coding change affects 36 firms and 143 firm-years out of 86,521 firm-years in the sample. Second, we code Georgia firms as not covered by a business combination law because the Georgia law requires that firms opt into coverage. This affects an additional 115 firms and 1,039 firm-years.20Additionally, we correct for six errors in the passage dates for business combination laws.21The result of these corrections is that the coefficient onBCfalls to −0.009, but it remains significant at the 1% level.
Model (4) reports the full model results that include our institutional and political economy control variables. The coefficient onBCis 0.000 and is statistically insignificant. In contrast, the coefficient onBC×MFis –0.297 and is significant at the 5% level. This result indicates that patent activity does decrease after passage of a business combination law when we control for the other effects, but only among the small subset of 25 firms that lobbied for business combination laws in the first place. This result is similar to several of the results in TableIV, in which the motivating firms experience significant changes around the adoption of business combination laws even when the coefficient onBCis insignificant.
In Model (4), firms’ patent activity is significantly negatively related toPoison pill lawandAmanda×BC. These results lead to substantially different inferences from the tests in columns (1) to (3), which focus only on business combination laws. If we maintain the assumption that coverage by business combination laws is a primary driver of a firm's takeover protection, these results contradict Atanassov's (2013) inference that patent activity is related to a firm's takeover protection. It is possible to maintain Atanassov's (2013) inference, but only if we also conclude that business combination laws do not, in and of themselves, provide substantial incremental takeover protection. Rather, the important drivers of firms’ takeover protection are poison pill laws and theAmandadecision. Furthermore, in robustness tests reported in SectionC.and the Internet Appendix, we find that the coefficient onAmanda×BCis not robust. For example, when we include a control for a nearly contemporaneous court decision that strengthened a firm's use of poison pills and a “just say no” takeover defense (Paramount Communications, Inc. v. Time, Inc.), the coefficient onParamount×Poison pill lawis negative and significant while the coefficient onAmanda×BCis insignificant. We infer that business combination laws are not significantly related to firms’ patent activity, while poison pill laws are.
Model (5) estimates the short regression for the subsample of firms in the Atanassov (2013) data that have prior firm-level defenses. Even in this truncated model, the coefficient onBCis statistically insignificant. This result is consistent with our results in TableV, which indicate that the incremental impact of a business combination law on the outcome variables is insignificant among firms with prior firm-level defenses.
Panel B of TableVIreplicates Atanassov's (2013) main result regarding the effects of business combination laws on citations to a firm's new patents. The dependent variable isLn(1 +Cit/Pat)t+3, which is the natural log of one plus the average number of citations per patent filed by the firm. Again, Atanassov (2013) uses a three-year leading citation measure to account for the fact that it takes time for research and development investments to yield patentable discoveries. TableVI, Model (6) replicates the central result in Atanassov's (2013) TableIII, Model (4). The coefficient onBCis –0.018 and statistically significant. The coefficient onBCremains significant in Model (7), where we use the reduced sample in which we can identify states of incorporation for the firm-years in Atanassov's (2013) sample, and in Model (8), where we adjust for opt-out firms and opt-in laws, as well as for six errors in the dates on which business combination laws were adopted.
Model (9) reports the results for the full model, which includes controls for other state antitakeover laws, court decisions, motivating firms, and firm-level defenses. The results are largely the same as in Panel A: patent citations are not significantly related to coverage by business combination laws but are negatively related to coverage by poison pill laws. Although patent citations are not significantly related to coverage by a business combination law for most firms, they are significantly related to coverage by a business combination law for the small number of firms that lobbied for these laws. Model (9) in Panel B reports a significant coefficient on the interactionAmanda×BC. As reported below, however, this result is not robust. We also observe a statistically significant but positive coefficient for coverage by a control share acquisition law. This result is not consistent with the joint hypothesis that control share acquisition laws provide exogenous takeover protection and firms respond to such protection by innovating less. We do not have a good explanation for this anomalous result.
In the Internet Appendix, we report results for a series of tests in which we examine the sensitivity of the TableVIresults to different assumptions about the lag between a firm's decision to invest in innovation and any resulting patents. Such sensitivity tests might be particularly important in our replication tests because our controls for other state laws and court decisions have the effect of parsing the sample observations into more and smaller time-related buckets. The robustness test results are consistent with the joint hypothesis that coverage by poison pill laws affects firms’ innovative activity and investments in innovation take three or four years to become apparent in the firm's patents. Throughout these tests, however, coverage by a business combination law is not related to patents or patent citations.
This section reports results for tests that examine the importance of two implicit assumptions in the specification of the tests reported in TablesIV–VI. The first assumption is about which court decisions had a material effect on firms’ takeover protections. Our tests include controls for four important court decisions that affect firms’ takeover protections. Cain, McKeon, and Solomon (2017), in contrast, identify three other court decisions as having potentially large effects on firms’ takeover defenses, and Catan and Kahan (2016) identify at least seven additional (typically lower) court decisions that potentially affect the legal status of state antitakeover laws and firms’ uses of poison pills. In the Internet Appendix, we report on tests that are analogous to those in TablesIVandVIthat include controls for these additional court decisions. With one exception, the additional court decisions are not significantly related to the outcome variables and their inclusion in the tests does not affect our other results. The exception involves the 1989 Delaware Supreme Court decision inParamount Communications, Inc. v. Time, Inc. (571 A.2d 1140, Del. Supr. 1989). TheParamountdecision affirmed the right of Time's board to implement its poison pill and “just say no” to Paramount's acquisition bid, rather than allow Time's shareholders to vote on Paramount's offer. When we include a control forParamount×Poison pill law, the coefficient is statistically significant for five of the nine outcome variables in TablesIVandVI. Furthermore, for the four outcome variables for whichAmanda×BCis significant in TablesIVandVI, the inclusion ofParamount×Poison pill lawmakes these coefficients insignificant. Both theAmandaandParamountdecisions were issued in 1989. We infer that theParamountcase had a substantive effect on many firms’ takeover protections, particularly for firms that are covered by poison pill laws, and that theAmanda×BCcoefficient picks up this effect in a few tests when theParamountcase is not included.
A second assumption in our test specification relates to which firms were affected by decisions rendered by the Delaware courts. Cain, McKeon, and Solomon (2017) report that 55% of the firm-years in their sample correspond to firms incorporated in Delaware, so Delaware court decisions directly affect the majority of large publicly traded firms. Delaware corporate law frequently leads, or becomes a template for, corporate law in other states. So, it is possible that Delaware court decisions have an extra-territorial impact on non-Delaware firms. The possibility of such an effect is particularly strong for the 1985Morandecision. Cremers and Ferrell (2014) argue thatMoranmay have provided legal sanction, or the likelihood of legal sanction, for poison pills by non-Delaware firms, even if those firms were incorporated in states that did not have poison pill laws. Catan and Kahan (2016) point out thatMoranmight have provided nationwide sanction for so-called flip-over poison pills, leaving the legal status of only more aggressive flip-in poison pills in doubt until subsequent court decisions and states’ adoption of poison pill laws.22
To explore this possibility, we replicate the tests in TablesIVandVIunder alternative assumptions about the firms impacted by theMorandecision. In one test, we assume that non-Delaware firms received 50% of the legal sanction to use poison pills as did Delaware firms.23We code thePoison pill lawvariable as increasing from 0 to 1 for all Delaware firms following theMorandecision, and from 0 to 0.5 for all non-Delaware firms that are not covered by poison pill laws. For firms that are subsequently covered by poison pill laws,Poison pill lawincreases from 0.5 to 1. The results of this alternate assumption about the reach and impact of theMorandecision are reported in the Internet Appendix. The effect is to increase the size and significance of the coefficient onPoison pill lawin some tests, as it becomes statistically significant when considering five of the nine outcome variables examined in TablesIVandVI. These results are consistent with the joint hypothesis that poison pill laws increased many firms’ takeover protections and that protections are related to various firm outcome variables.
article.VII. Comments on an Extreme View of Shad:
In related papers, Catan and Kahan (2016) and Cain, McKeon, and Solomon (2017) also pose questions about the use of state antitakeover laws to identify exogenous changes in firms’ takeover protections. Like our paper, both of these studies argue that state antitakeover laws are not adopted in an institutional vacuum and that legal context matters. Cain, McKeon, and Solomon (2017) construct a “Takeover Index” using fitted values from a hostile acquisition likelihood model that includes controls for several court decisions and state antitakeover laws, macroeconomic conditions, and firm characteristics. While the Takeover Index itself is subject to critique—for instance, it does not approximate an exogenous measure of takeover protection because it includes endogenous firm characteristics and it may not generalize to acquisitions not classified as hostile—it nonetheless provides a measure of a firm's takeover protection that considers a broad set of state laws and court decisions.
Catan and Kahan's (2016) critique of the literature is more sweeping. Catan and Kahan (CK) build upon observation in Coates (2000) and Klausner (2013) that virtually all firms have the ability to adopt poison pills at any time, even after receiving an unsolicited takeover bid. So, even firms that do not explicitly have poison pills have latent, or shadow, pills. Shadow poison pills are particularly effective deterrents when combined with classified boards because a classified board can force a bidder to win two consecutive board elections to gain a board majority, repeal the poison pill, and complete the acquisition. We agree with CK's argument that shadow poison pills should have a substantial effect on firms’ takeover protections, and some of our empirical results (showing that poison pill endorsement laws are significantly related to several firm outcomes) are consistent with this argument. But a reviewer of our paper points out that it is possible to push CK's insight to an extreme conclusion, namely, that shadow pills combine with classified boards to make virtually all other defenses redundant, such that state antitakeover laws imposenoincremental takeover protection. Such an extreme view of shadow poison pills implies that any empirical result linking an outcome variable to the adoption of a state antitakeover law must be spurious, as theoretically it cannot be attributed to a change in firms’ takeover protections.
We believe this extreme view is unreliable as a guide to research in this area. To hold, it would require that poison pills are costless for managers to implement and, if combined with classified boards, offer takeover protection that is so unambiguously effective as to render other types of defenses irrelevant. However, theory and evidence indicate that poison pills are not costless to deploy and that other types of takeover defenses do offer incremental takeover protection. Our paper shows that institutional and legal context matters for research that uses state antitakeover laws for identification. But our results do not support the extreme view that such laws provide no test identification, and we reject the notion that all empirical relations between state antitakeover laws and firm outcomes must be spurious. Rather, our paper demonstrates how one might better specify tests that meaningfully exploit the variation in takeover protections afforded by state antitakeover laws.
To elaborate on our position, in the rest of this section we argue that an extreme application of CK's insight is unwarranted because it: (i) ignores equilibrium considerations, (ii) presents an oversimplified view of poison pills and other defenses, and (iii) is inconsistent with available evidence.
(i)Equilibrium Considerations. It is difficult to reconcile simple observation with the assertion that poison pills are so effective as to render all other defenses irrelevant. Courts first upheld the use of poison pills in the mid- and late-1980s, so the extreme view implies that firms should have abandoned all defenses other than classified boards since then. However, states have adopted at least 136 antitakeover laws since 1985, and firms have not abandoned defenses other than classified boards. Indeed, Straska and Waller (2014) document that most types of defenses have become more—not less—widespread since the late 1980s. The use of takeover defenses has increased especially among initial public offering (IPO) firms (e.g., see Daines and Klausner (2001), Field and Karpoff (2002), Johnson, Karpoff, and Yi (2015)). Clearly, managers and legislators think these various defenses are relevant. An equilibrium in which these defenses are misguided but neutral mutations is difficult to square with the fact that their use is widespread, persistent, and increasing over time.
Many types of state antitakeover laws and firm-level takeover defenses affect firm value, implying that investors also regard them as relevant (e.g., Karpoff and Malatesta (1989), Gompers, Ishii, and Metrick (2003), Bebchuk, Cohen, and Ferrell (2009), Cremers and Ferrell (2014), Johnson, Karpoff, and Yi (2017)). It is possible to argue that investors’ valuations of various takeover defenses are simply wrong and that investors should care only about classified boards. But such a claim requires that investors systematically and perversely misestimate the value impacts of takeover defenses and have not learned despite decades of experience—another implausible argument.
Bidders are also concerned about the deterrence afforded by state antitakeover laws. It is common, for example, for a bidder to condition its tender offer on the target board's decision to opt out of the provisions of an antitakeover law or a judicial determination that the law would not apply.24Consistent with this observation, Subramanian, Herscovici, and Barbetta (2010a) argue that the evidence indicates that Delaware's business combination law has afforded some takeover deterrence.25
A key reason the extreme view fails is that it is based on an implicit assumption that managers face no tradeoff, as poison pills are assumed to be extremely effective yet costless. This assumption is inconsistent with observations that some firms do not use poison pills and, in recent years, nearly all pills are adopted with sunset provisions (Catan (2017)). The assumption is at odds with evidence that managers who deploy poison pills suffer reputational losses (Bednar, Love, and Kraatz (2015)), that directors of firms deploying pills lose votes in subsequent board elections (Johnson, Karpoff, and Wittry (2017)), and that decreasing vote support is correlated with negative career consequences for both managers and directors—all indications of costly consequences for firms and managers.26Firms and directors deploying poison pills also face adverse recommendations from shareholder advisory services (e.g., Institutional Shareholder Services (2015)). Even if shareholder advisory services have conflicting incentives (e.g., as argued by Choi, Fisch, and Kahan (2010)), their adverse recommendations typically impose costs on incumbent managers and directors.
All of these considerations indicate that the extreme claim that only shadow poison pills matter is based on inaccurate assumptions. Contrary to such a claim, multiple types of defenses are common and consequential, and poison pills are not costless to deploy. Stated differently, the equilibrium use of takeover defenses is not best characterized as a corner solution in which all firms have poison pills (and perhaps classified boards) and no other defenses. Rather, poison pills and other takeover defenses confer both costs and benefits, and firms are likely to find interior solutions in which they deploy a variety of takeover defenses precisely because each one affords some incremental takeover protection (and possibly other benefits). One reason state antitakeover laws can be particularly effective is that the state, as opposed to managers and directors, bears much of the cost of the defense.
(ii)Theoretical Oversimplifications. The claim that shadow poison pills make all defenses other than classified boards irrelevant ignores important details about how pills and other takeover defenses can interact and affect the interpretation of empirical tests. For example, there remains substantial ambiguity about exactly which events led to the widespread recognition that all firms have shadow poison pills. One candidate is the 1985Morandecision, which upheld a Delaware firm's use of a poison pill.27Subramanian (2002), however, suggests that firms’ rights to deploy pills were better secured by the 1989Paramountdecision.28Cain, McKeon, and Solomon (2017) argue that the 1990Great Northern Nekoosadecision was central,29whereas Rauh (2006) argues that a combination of mid-1990s Delaware court decisions was key.30Even if we assume that most firms’ rights to shadow pills were secure by the mid-1990s, these rights were not recognized in the legal literature until 2000.31This timing matters because many studies examine the impact of state antitakeover laws using samples that include years between 1985 and 2000. So, even if one accepts that shadow poison pills trump all other defenses, inferences based on results from the 1980s will be unaffected if shadow pills were not recognized until 1995 or 2000.32
Even the discussion here understates the complexity of the underlying legal issues, as Delaware courts’ views of poison pills have evolved over time.
