The 1985Morandecision considered only a flip-over pill, which the court regarded as relatively benign. Reflecting much legal uncertainty moving forward, a 1988 Delaware Chancery Court struck down a poison pill defense (City Capital Associates v. Interco Inc., 551 A.2d 787) only to be overruled by the Delaware Supreme Court in the 1989Paramountdecision. Edelman and Thomas (2012) argue that the key ambiguity regarding a pill's legal status is whether it “fundamentally restricts” a bidder's ability to wage a successful proxy contest to gain control (i.e., is “preclusive”). Using this screen, Edelman and Thomas (2012) argue that a pill's legal status has been shaped mostly by the 1985Unocal, 1995Unitrin, and 2010Selecticadecisions, implying that shadow pills’ effect on takeover deterrence is not absolute and has been strongest only since 2010.33(As discussed in SectionC., we examine these issues in our empirical tests by considering the possibility that earlier court decisions offered some, but not certain, assurance about firms’ access to poison pills.) Bebchuk and Jackson (2014) argue that poison pills’ legal status has never been solidified and that pills are vulnerable to legal challenge based on their preemption by the 1968 Williams Act. This claim contradicts the assumption behind the extreme view that pills unambiguously and completely shield managers from the risk of an unwanted takeover bid.
Catan and Kahan's (2016) argument regarding shadow poison pills supports claims that classified boards are the only defense that conveys incremental takeover protection (e.g., Bates, Becher, and Lemmon (2008)). Again, we agree that classified boards and poison pills combine to offer a strong takeover defense. But this insight does not imply that no other defenses matter. One reason other defenses can be important is simply that many firms do not have classified boards.34But even for firms with classified boards, other types of defenses can increase a firm's takeover protection. Restrictions on shareholders’ ability to call special meetings or to act by written consent, for example, can force a delay in a bidder's ability to remove existing directors, much like a classified board. The presence of blank check preferred stock can facilitate the quick adoption of a poison pill, so firms without blank check preferred stock may face higher costs of adopting a pill. As another example, supermajority vote requirements make it easier for minority blockholders to block a merger or increase its cost by holding out for a higher price (e.g., see Boone, Broughman, and Macias (2017)).35
Similarly, state antitakeover laws logically impose incremental hurdles for bidders even for firms with poison pills. As discussed in SectionB., poison pill endorsement laws were motivated in part by uncertainty over poison pills’ legal status. By partially resolving uncertainty over pills’ legal status, these laws increase firms’ takeover protections. Directors’ duties statutes provide legal cover for “just say no” defenses, including those that use poison pills, thus decreasing the risk of litigation over the firm's defensive efforts. Control share acquisition laws require supermajority votes to approve a bid offer and thus theoretically increase the risk that a takeover bid could be thwarted by a holdout by minority shareholders. Catan and Kahan (2016, p. 639) argue that poison pills render business combination laws redundant because modern pills’ trigger thresholds are low (e.g., 10% of shares) and preclude a bidder from ever acquiring the ownership stake required to trigger the provisions of the business combination law. But this argument ignores the same logic CK apply to classified boards. Just like a classified board can theoretically impose incremental costs on a bidder by extending the time to rescind a pill and effect the takeover, a business combination law's two- to five-year forced delay can extend the time before a bidder can consummate an acquisition even further.
Thus, the same type of theoretical argument used to advance an extreme claim based on CK's insight also undermines that claim, as many other types of firm- and state-level takeover defenses theoretically confer incremental takeover protections for firms. Clearly, not all takeover defenses offer equal or substantial incremental takeover protections. But our argument that many defenses can offer incremental takeover protections is much less demanding than the extreme claim that only poison pills and classified boards offer any incremental takeover protection.
(iii)Available Evidence. We agree with CK's argument that some prior findings reflect model misspecification—indeed, the results in SectionVI.of this paper demonstrate this point.36But one would have to maintain unrealistically strong priors to therefore infer that all previous results are spurious (i.e., those listed in TableA1), as well as the subset of our results in SectionVI.that are robust to the inclusion of controls for legal context. Our tests include high-dimensional firm, industry, and time fixed effects, thus negating CK's implication that the omission of firm or time fixed effects explains most previous results. In addition, at least 13 recent papers use versions of our legal context controls and find that state antitakeover laws are significantly related to various firm outcomes.37
An extreme interpretation of CK's insight is also refuted by direct evidence on the impact of various takeover defenses on firms’ takeover protection. Karpoff, Schonlau, and Wehrly (2017) find that, accounting for endogeneity, takeover defenses in the G-index other than classified boards offer effective takeover defenses. A number of papers also find stronger results using broad indices of takeover defenses rather than using only classified boards, or using the G-index instead of the E-index. These results all indicate that takeover defenses other than classified boards are significantly related to important firm outcomes.38
One goal of this paper is to establish the importance of institutional and legal context in tests that use legal changes for identification. We argue that it is naïve to assume that any particular second-generation state antitakeover law provides good test identification without considering firms’ coverage by first-generation laws, other second-generation laws, firm-level defenses, and the endogenous process by which the law came about. However, both theory and evidence indicate that it would be equally misleading to swing to an opposite extreme by asserting that state antitakeover laws provide no identification. That is, state antitakeover laws can still identify plausibly exogenous changes in firms’ takeover protections, as long as researchers take care to consider the complexity of the institutional and legal context in which these laws are enacted.
article.VIII. Conclusions and Implications for F:
This paper examines the use of regulatory and legal changes to identify exogenous variation in empirical tests and argues that the institutional and legal context of such changes can have a large effect on inferences. We focus on a popular strategy whereby state antitakeover laws are used to identify exogenous changes in firms’ takeover vulnerability. Our theoretical discussion and empirical results indicate that tests that ignore the broader legal context are misspecified, as it is important to control for coverage by both first- and second-generation laws, firm-level defenses, major court decisions that affect the legality and impact of antitakeover laws, and the endogenous process through which the laws are passed. We conclude with a discussion of the implications of these results for future research.
Our overall argument is that observational data used in empirical tests are profoundly affected by the institutional, political economy, and legal context in which people and firms operate, and that test power and accuracy can be improved by incorporating controls for such context. We demonstrate this general argument in tests that use business combination or other antitakeover laws for identification by including controls that reflect the institutional and legal context in which the laws are adopted. In particular, the long equation models in TablesIVandVIinclude controls for first-generation laws, other second-generation laws, important court decisions, and motivating firms. As discussed in SectionVI., the results for several outcome variables are sensitive to the inclusion of these additional controls. However, not all prior results are sensitive to the inclusion of our controls for legal context. For example, our long equation results support some of the inferences in Bertrand and Mullainathan (2003, regarding asset growth), Giroud and Mueller (2010, regarding SGA expenses), Wald and Long (2007, regarding leverage), and John and Litov (2010, regarding leverage).
Most observers agree that takeover defenses are not all alike (e.g., see Larcker, Reiss, and Xiao (2015)). However, prior discussions of which defenses are most important remain exercises in theory and assertion. For example, our SectionII.discussion of which type of state antitakeover law is “most stringent” is devoid of empirical measurement, as the extreme view of shadow poison pills that we discuss in SectionVII.. Such theoretical discussions can help, but empirical research is required to establish which specific antitakeover provisions, laws, and court decisions most affect firms’ takeover protection—and therefore, which are good candidates to use in empirical tests. A related question is how various types of defenses interact to provide incremental takeover deterrence. For example, some types of takeover protections may complement or substitute for each other, implying that the combination of a business combination law and poison pill law could have an effect on takeover deterrence that differs from the sum of the partial effects of each law.
As first steps, Bebchuk, Cohen, and Ferrell (2009) appeal to some empirical findings in arguing for the importance of their E-index provisions. Bates, Becher, and Lemmon (2008) and Kadyrzhanova and Rhodes-Kropf (2011) provide evidence suggesting that classified boards may be negatively related to acquisition likelihood. Cain, McKeon, and Solomon (2017) test whether the 1968 Williams Act and various types of state laws and court decisions are related to hostile acquisition likelihood. Karpoff, Schonlau, and Wehrly (2017) show that the G-index, E-index, and O-index (G-index provisions not in the E-index) all are negatively related to acquisition likelihood in tests that account for their endogenous adoption. The empirical results in SectionVI.of this paper can be interpreted as tentatively identifying several laws and court decisions that also affect firms’ takeover defenses, including poison pill endorsement laws, firm-level defenses, and perhaps business combination laws after the 1989Amandacourt decision.
Until more progress is made in empirically establishing which laws, court decisions, and specific takeover defenses confer incremental takeover protection, and under what circumstances, research in this area may best proceed inductively. As an example, in SectionB.we infer that it is possible to maintain Atanassov's (2013) conclusion—that increased protection from takeovers leads firms to innovate less—but only if we abandon the assumption that business combination laws identify a significant increase in firms’ takeover defenses. Rather than business combination laws, our results suggest that poison pill laws and the 1989Amandacourt decision worked to increase takeover defenses. Such a conclusion is an example of inductive, as opposed to deductive, inference.
Most empirical finance research is presented as an application of deductive logic in which the researcher develops testable hypotheses from an established theoretical framework. Inductive reasoning, in which the most plausible inference is drawn from the data, lacks the apparent logical structure of deductive reasoning and is subject to concerns about data mining and ex post theorizing (e.g., see Allen (2001)). In practice, however, much of science proceeds through induction. For example, Kell and Oliver (2003) point out that genome sequencing programs generally advanced through induction. Fisher (1955, p. 74) argues that such inductive advances are the norm, as “Deductive reasoning … supplies no essentially new knowledge, but merely reveals or unfolds the implications of the axiomatic basis adopted. Ideally, perhaps, it should be carried out mechanically. It is the function of inductive reasoning to be used, in conjunction with observational data, to add new elements to our theoretical knowledge.” Arthur (1994), Clower (1994), and Johnson (1996) apply this argument to economics, which, they maintain, is best understood as an inductive science. Along these lines, McCloskey (1986, p. 64) criticizes economists for “cling[ing] to a quaint positivism…”. Discussing how most economic discovery occurs, Arthur (1994, p. 411) argues that, “as humans in these contexts we useinductivereasoning: we induce a variety of working hypotheses, act upon the most credible, and replace hypotheses with new ones if they cease to work.” Clower (1994, p. 810) adds that, “Inductive sciences deal with plausible inference, not with demonstrative reasoning….”
Induction already plays a prominent role in finance. For example, the identification of factor structures to model expected stock returns typically does not rely on ex ante hypotheses, but rather an ex post observation (see Harvey, Liu, and Zhu (2016)). Given the current lack of evidence about which laws and court decisions best identify exogenous shocks to firms’ takeover protections, research in this area may best proceed, at least in the short run, by embracing an inductive approach to empirical inference in tests that examine the effects of firm- and state-level takeover protections on firm operations and performance.

article.Appendix A: Descriptions of the Five Maj:
Business combination laws: These laws, also called freeze-out laws, impose a moratorium on significant asset sales or mergers between a large shareholder and the covered firm once the large shareholder's stake passes a threshold level. For example, the New York business combination law prohibits asset sales or a merger with a 20% shareholder for five years. Even after the moratorium, most business combination laws allow the business combination to proceed only if the transaction satisfies fair price provisions. Thus, the typical business combination law is like a fair price law with a forced delay. Under most such laws, the provisions of the law can be relaxed if the business combination is preapproved by shareholders or the target firm's board of directors.
Control share acquisition laws: This law requires shareholder approval before a large shareholder may vote shares obtained in a control share acquisition. For example, Indiana's control share acquisition law defines a control share acquisition as a series of acquisitions over time that, without the law, would increase a large shareholder's share of total voting rights to 20%, 33 1/3%, or 50%. To obtain the voting rights associated with the control shares, the large shareholder must receive approval from the majority of all disinterested shares (i.e., shares not owned by the large shareholder or officers of the firm). Many control share acquisition laws are patterned after the Indiana law because, in 1987, the U.S. Supreme Court upheld the Indiana law inCTS v. Dynamics Corp. of America.
Directors’ duties laws: Also called constituency laws, these laws explicitly expand board members’ duties to act in the best interests of the company, to include the interests of noninvestor stakeholders. The effect is to provide legal authorization to justify decisions that do not serve shareholders’ interests. Pennsylvania's directors’ duties law, for example, states that “… In determining the best interests of the corporation, a director may consider: (1) the interests of the corporation's shareholders, employees, customers, creditors, suppliers, and communities in which it is located; (2) the long-term and short-term interests of the corporation, including the possibility that these interests may be best served by its continued independence… The board shall not be required to consider the interests of any particular group as dominant or controlling…or take other action solely because of the effect it might have on the consideration that might be paid to shareholders in an acquisition.”
Fair price laws: These laws impose restrictions that are similar to fair price provisions adopted by many firms. The law regulates the back-end price in a two-tiered takeover bid or other significant business combination involving a large shareholder. The typical fair price law prohibits business combinations between the firm and a large stockholder unless one of two conditions is met. Either (1) prior approval is granted by a supermajority (e.g., 80%) of all outstanding voting stock and by a supermajority (e.g., two-thirds) of the outstanding stock not held by the interested stockholder, or (2) stockholders receive a stipulated price for the stock acquired by the large stockholder as part of the business combination. The stipulated price is set by a formula that guarantees the price paid will be very high.
Poison pill laws: These laws, also known as poison pill endorsement laws, grant firms that are covered by the law the right to adopt poison pill takeover defenses. For example, Virginia's poison pill law authorizes a corporation to “… issue rights, options or warrants for the purchase of shares of the company upon such terms and conditions and for such consideration, if any, and such purposes as may be approved by the board of directors.” Poison pill laws can be important because the right to use a poison pill defense is more secure when explicitly authorized by statute, and is less likely to be limited by court action.
